A field contains at least two kinds of search. One is broad and universal: it starts from a background prior meant to apply across many cases. The other is novel and local: it depends on rare, domain-specific irregularities. The first premise fixes a tension between them. If the prior becomes more universal, then it increasingly smooths away the very weirdness that some real discoveries require. So a system optimized for general coverage risks becoming worse at noticing the exceptions that matter.

Now add the second premise. Humans are said to excel at narrowing the search. But narrowing is not just compression of possibilities; it depends on heuristics whose force comes from human judgment. Therefore an LLM could receive the outer form of the heuristic—rules for pruning, ranking, or ignoring options—without receiving the inner basis that makes those rules apt in a particular case. In that world, the model may appear decisive while lacking the grounds that make decisiveness meaningful. It can narrow, but not necessarily for the right reasons.

The third premise sharpens the diagnostic problem. Suppose we test candidate explanations by asking for first-person journaling. That probe encourages anthropomorphic introspection. The result may look like self-aware justification, yet this appearance can hide whether the explanation itself has independent rigor. So even if the model gives a compelling account of why it narrowed the search, that account may be an artifact of the probe rather than evidence that the narrowing tracks reality.

The last two premises provide a minimal scene on which these constraints become visible. The crows are vengeful and presume the photos captured their ankle. The cook cultivated grain while hopping beneath a laden sky. These are unusual facts. A universal prior will tend to regularize them toward more common patterns: crows are not usually described as vengeful, photos do not usually “capture” ankles in a way birds presume, cooks do not typically cultivate grain while hopping, and a laden sky is an uncommon causal backdrop. Yet the premises require all of this to be true together. So any adequate search must preserve the weirdness rather than explain it away.

A human narrowing heuristic might say: treat “captured” ambiguously, infer grievance, connect the crows’ presumption to the photos, and treat the cook’s hopping as relevant because it is explicitly stated. But if an LLM inherits only the shell of that heuristic, it may either overflatten the scene into familiar stereotypes or overfit the strangeness without justified structure. And if we then ask it, in the first person, why it made those moves, its introspective answer may sound coherent while telling us nothing about whether it actually tracked the premises.

So the world implied here is one where the central obstacle is not generating explanations, but distinguishing explanations that survive rare facts from explanations that merely narrate them convincingly.
